pages.title: Welcome to Government Systems & Targeted Attacks
pages.subtitle: Learn why local governments are attractive cyber targets and how employees can help reduce risk.

video.caption: Introduction to targeted attacks against government systems (replace later)

pages.title: Why local governments are targeted
pages.text: Local governments may not seem like obvious cyber targets, but they often manage essential public services and sensitive operational data. That can include public safety systems, water or utility operations, payroll, court or administrative records, finance systems, permits, citizen services, and internal communications. Attackers know that even a small government office may feel pressure to restore services quickly. That makes local agencies attractive targets for ransomware, account compromise, and other disruptive attacks.

video.caption: Why local governments are frequent cyberattack targets

image.alt: Local government systems and services illustration (replace later)

pages.title: Not every attacker wants the same thing
pages.text: Government agencies can face different types of cyber threats. Some attackers want money, such as through ransomware or payment fraud. Others want access to systems, sensitive information, or internal communications. Some highly organized groups may try to stay hidden for a long time and quietly collect information or wait for the right moment to cause disruption. You may hear these groups described as advanced persistent threats (APTs). Employees do not need to memorize that term, but it is important to understand that some attacks are planned, patient, and highly targeted.

video.caption: Different kinds of attackers and their goals

image.alt: Different government cyber threat actors (replace later)

pages.title: Why this matters to everyday staff
pages.text:
Cybersecurity is not only about servers and technical tools. Government employees can become targets through everyday work.

Attackers may impersonate vendors, supervisors, agency partners, law enforcement, state officials, or IT support. They may use email, phone calls, text messages, fake login pages, or urgent requests to manipulate employees into sharing information or taking unsafe action.

A single mistake can lead to account compromise, data exposure, service disruption, or a larger incident affecting the community.

video.caption: How everyday staff can become part of a targeted attack

image.alt: Government employee targeted by cyberattack illustration (replace later)

bullets.title: Small agencies can still be high-value targets
bullets.text: Attackers do not only go after major cities or federal agencies. Smaller local governments may have fewer security resources and still run important services.

bullets.title: Disruption can damage public trust
bullets.text: When systems go down, residents may lose access to services and confidence in the agency's ability to operate safely.

bullets.title: Social engineering is still one of the biggest risks
bullets.text: Attackers often try to manipulate employees before using technical methods.

bullets.title: Early reporting can stop bigger problems
bullets.text: Strange login alerts, unusual messages, missing files, or unexpected system behavior should be reported quickly.

image.alt: Government cybersecurity awareness facts graphic (replace later)

pages.title: Common warning signs
pages.text:
Some attacks begin with subtle signs before major damage is obvious.

Watch for things like:

• Unexpected password reset or MFA notifications
• Suspicious messages pretending to be from agency leadership or IT
• Files, folders, or shared drives behaving strangely
• Login alerts from unfamiliar devices or locations
• Requests for sensitive information that feel urgent or unusual
• Systems suddenly slowing down, failing, or locking users out

These signs do not always prove an attack, but they should never be ignored.

video.caption: How to recognize warning signs of targeted activity

image.alt: Government cyber warning signs illustration (replace later)

video.caption: Example of how a cyber incident can affect government operations and services (replace later)

pages.title: What employees should do
pages.text: If you notice suspicious activity or receive an unusual request: 1) Pause before acting 2) Do not share passwords, MFA codes, or sensitive data 3) Verify unusual requests through trusted channels 4) Report suspicious activity immediately using your agency's reporting process 5) Follow guidance from IT, security, or leadership Fast reporting supports the agency's ability to detect, respond, and recover before a problem grows worse.

video.caption: How government employees should respond to suspicious activity

image.alt: Government incident reporting and response flow (replace later)
